report_markdown:
# Your Week: April 7–11, 2026

14 hours of meetings this week. Your longest uninterrupted focus block was 1.5 hours. You completed 2 of 11 tasks — an 18% completion rate — and sent 4 emails after hours. That's the week in numbers.

## The Front-Half Collapse

Monday hit a load score of 95 out of 100. You had 5 meetings that day, contributing to 6 back-to-back sessions across the week, and you completed zero tasks on Monday, Tuesday, and Wednesday combined. Wednesday ran a 7pm meeting on top of 4 others. By the time Thursday arrived with just 1 meeting and a load score of 38, the week was already decided. You didn't have a bad week — you had a catastrophic Monday and Wednesday that made the rest largely irrelevant.

## The Avoidance Pattern

Your personal tasks were moved 4 times despite only 3 existing — more reschedules than actual tasks. Financial tasks: 2 tasks, 0 completed, moved 3 times. You have 94 unread emails and 11 unanswered threads sitting untouched. This isn't falling behind — this is a consistent decision, repeated daily, to look away from a specific category of demands. Personal tasks were flagged as your most-avoided category, and the data backs that up completely.

## The Boundary Problem

You started Monday with an 8am meeting and ended Wednesday at 7pm with another. Four late-night email sends happened across the week. You had exactly 1 free evening in 5 days. Your day has no protected edges right now — neither end is holding, which means recovery isn't happening at the margins where it normally would.

## The Spending Signal

You spent $110 on food delivery against a week where you also spent $67.20 on groceries. Food delivery alone is your highest single category. Total spend hit $340.57 — 58.3% of monthly budget in one week. When the calendar is this compressed and evenings are gone, spending patterns shift toward convenience and away from intention. The $110 isn't random; it's what a 95-load-score Monday looks like by dinnertime.

**The root cause: your calendar is being set by other people's priorities, and everything else — tasks, boundaries, evenings, spending — is absorbing the consequences.**
root_cause: Your calendar is being set by other people's priorities, and everything else — tasks, boundaries, evenings, spending — is absorbing the consequences.
